The thymus is most active during childhood, but continues to play a role in immune regulation in adults, though it shrinks with age and produces less thymus hormones like thymosin which support immune balance. The thymus gland plays a central role in immune system education, helping immune cells distinguish between the body’s own cells and harmful invaders. Disorders of the thymus can lead to autoimmune diseases or a weakened immune response. Diagnosis involves medical history, physical exams, imaging (like chest X-rays or CT scans), and blood tests.
